coredata.dc:title: Efficient solar-driven photothermoelectric generator facilitated by carbon dots aggregates
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Shan, Feishi
Yan, Li
Wei, Zhihong
Liao, Chengshuang
Li, Jing
Wang, Zhouyu
Tian, Yuxi
Wang, Leyong
coredata.dc:description: The energy crisis is a significant issue that the world is facing in the 21st century. Thermoelectric energy transfer emerges as a promising solution that can convert heat energy into electric energy. However, there remains a big challenge that the efficient utilization of light energy to achieve continuous electricity ultimately. The red emitting carbon dots (RCDs) could self-assemble into the aggregates (ARCDs) via cooperative solvophobic effect combined with hydrogen bonding interaction, which exhibits considerably enhanced absorption with excellent photothermal conversion effect (PCE ≈ 62%) compared to its dispersed state. Moreover, these aggregates were then loaded onto semiconductors to create solar-driven photothermoelectric generator (LHE), with unprecedented output efficiency (Voutput ≈ 5000 mV, Ioutput ≈ 25 mA, and Poutput ≈ 123 mW), which can easily charge smartphones outdoors. This work offers a supramolecular chemistry perspective for the construction of CDs aggregates and presents an sustainable approach towards achieving continuous photothermoelectric energy transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogen bond
Solvophobic effect
Aggregates
Photothermoelectric generator

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871136), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1977, 2022ZYD0048), Sichuan Provincial College Student Innovation Training Program (S202210623049, 202310623017), and the Starry Night Science Fund of Zhejiang University Shanghai Institute for Advanced Study, Grant No. SN-ZJU-SIAS-006. We thank Mr. Chengquan Shan, a veteran electricity enthusiast, for his help in making the LHE conversion model. We thank Chengdu Zhichuangxin Advertising Co. Ltd for the manufacture of LED Lamp too.

This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871136 ), Sichuan Science and Technology Program ( 2023NSFSC1977 , 2022ZYD0048 ), Sichuan Provincial College Student Innovation Training Program ( S202210623049 , 202310623017 ), and the Starry Night Science Fund of Zhejiang University Shanghai Institute for Advanced Study , Grant No. SN-ZJU-SIAS-006 . We thank Mr. Chengquan Shan, a veteran electricity enthusiast, for his help in making the LHE conversion model. We thank Chengdu Zhichuangxin Advertising Co., Ltd for the manufacture of LED Lamp too.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The absorption spectra (300−800 nm) of RCDs dispersed in H2O and DMSO (298 K) at a concentration of ∼20 μg/mL (inset: digital photographs of different concentrations of RCDs, (i) in H2O; (ii) in DMSO). (b) The absorption spectra (300−800 nm) of RCDs dispersed in DMSO (298 K) with different concentration (inset: partial enlarged diagram (550−730 nm) in Fig. 1(b)). (c) The PL spectra of RCDs dispersed in DMSO (298 K) (inset: PL intensity trends with emission peaks of 600 nm and 660 nm at different concentration). TEM/HRTEM image and the diameter distribution histogram of RCDs dispersed in (d) DMSO (1 μg/mL), (e) DMSO (5 μg/mL). (f) Potential explanation for RCDs aggregating in DMSO is that unaggregated RCDs progressively gathered as concentration rose due to the combined action of solvophobic effect and H-bonding interactions.

(a) 3D pseudo color map (in bird's eye) of transient absorption (TA) spectra of RCDs in DMSO (10 μg/mL). (b) TA spectra of RCDs in DMSO (10 μg/mL) at indicated delay times. (c) Kinetic traces of RCDs in DMSO (10 μg/mL) at different probe wavelength. (d) Preparation process of ARCDs: freeze-dried DMSO solution of RCDs (DMSO bound with RCDs are represented by the blue molecules, whereas DMSO free of RCDs are non-blue). (e) XPS survey spectrum (175–155 eV) of RCDs (red trace) & ARCDs (blue trace) showing S 2p. (f) SAXS patterns of RCDs (red curve) and ARCDs (blue curve). (g) FTIR spectra of RCDs (red curve) & ARCDs (blue curve). (h) DSC profiles of RCDs (red curve) & ARCDs (blue curve).

(a) Thermographic images of ARCDs with different concentrations irradiated by 808 nm laser (∼1 W/cm2) for 10 min; (b) Thermographic images of ARCDs (c=160 μg/mL) irradiated with different 808 nm laser power for 10 min; (c) Data integration histograms for (a) & (b). (d) Photothermal stability performance of ARCDs over four cycles of laser on/off upon 808 nm irradiation at a power density of 1 W/cm2.

(a) Schematic diagram of the copolymerization of PA and RCDs to construct photothermal materials (ARCDs@PA); (b) SEM images of ARCDs@PA (scale bar, 100 μm) (inset: pore morphology on ARCDs@PA (scale bar, 10 μm)); (c) TEM images of ARCDs@PA (scale bar, 500 nm; 200 nm) and the diameter distribution histogram of ARCDs; (d) Absorbance of PA (red trace) and ARCDs@PA (blue trace) overlaid on the solar spectrum (gray trace); (e) Temperature difference among blank, PA and ARCDs@PA under the same illumination condition (inset: thermal image of blank, PA, and ARCDs@PA); (f) The working principle of the solar-driven photothermoelectric generator (LHE) and the schematic diagram of voltage and current test; (g) The two LHEs in series were positioned outdoors on lawn and mimics a power station (inset: voltage values (top left), current values (bottom left) and digital photos of mobile phone charging by LHEs (right)). (h) The output power stability of the LHEs over 10 cycles of Xe lamp on/off at a power density of 100 mW/cm2; (i) Performance comparison between LHE and other photothermoelectric generator.

Following the aggregation of RCDs, a schematic diagram demonstrates a significant improvement in photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE). Additionally, the combination photothermal and thermoelectric conversion of solar-driven photothermoelectric generator (LHE) constructed by blending ARCDs with PA and then loading them on semiconductor is shown, ultimately achieving outdoor smartphone charging.

head.ce:title: Efficient solar-driven photothermoelectric generator facilitated by carbon dots aggregates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The energy crisis is a significant issue that the world is facing in the 21st century. Thermoelectric energy transfer emerges as a promising solution that can convert heat energy into electric energy. However, there remains a big challenge that the efficient utilization of light energy to achieve continuous electricity ultimately. The red emitting carbon dots (RCDs) could self-assemble into the aggregates (ARCDs) via cooperative solvophobic effect combined with hydrogen bonding interaction, which exhibits considerably enhanced absorption with excellent photothermal conversion effect (PCE ≈ 62%) compared to its dispersed state. Moreover, these aggregates were then loaded onto semiconductors to create solar-driven photothermoelectric generator (LHE), with unprecedented output efficiency (Voutput ≈ 5000 mV, Ioutput ≈ 25 mA, and Poutput ≈ 123 mW), which can easily charge smartphones outdoors. This work offers a supramolecular chemistry perspective for the construction of CDs aggregates and presents an sustainable approach towards achieving continuous photothermoelectric energy transfer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Collective Sparks Ignite Brighter Flames: Solar-driven CDs aggregates enable efficient photothermal conversion, combined with thermoelectric materials to achieve continuous light-heat-electricity energy transfer—offering a practical approach to address energy challenges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: World Energy Outlook 2023, issued by the International Energy Agency (IEA), highlights the unprecedented, profound, and complex energy crisis the world is currently facing. The depletion of traditional fossil fuels and the significant emission of greenhouse gases are insufficient to meet the urgent needs of improving human living standards and advancing modern industrialization [1,2]. In recent years, researchers have shown great interest in the direct conversion of heat into electric energy through the Seebeck effect [3,4]. This method offers continuous supply, requires no maintenance, and is environmentally friendly [5]. Additionally, photothermal conversion, which is a fundamental process in nature, is widely observed in physical, chemical, and biological reactions [6]. Materials can effectively convert light into heat energy by absorbing photons from sunlight, thus harnessing renewable solar energy [7–9]. However, the development of a photothermoelectric generator, which enables continuous transfer from light, to heat, further to electric energy, remains a major challenge in the field of new energy. The most critical factor in this development is achieving efficient photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE).

Quantum dots (QDs) are a good medium for photothermal conversion [10–13]. Carbon dots (CDs), regarded as a new branch of QDs, are well-known for their non-toxicity, strong modifiability, and excellent photothermal conversion ability [14–19]. In contrast to conventional carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotubes, MXenes), CDs demonstrate significantly simpler synthesis process with reduced energy demands and enhanced environmental compatibility, while featuring a characteristic core-shell structure where the carbon core predominantly comprises sp²/sp³-hybridized carbon atoms and high-density heteroatoms [20–23]. Multivariate heterocyclic lattices are widely present in heteroatom-doped carbon cores, which contain a large number of free electrons capable of spontaneously forming delocalized π bonds. These electrons transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) when excited by photons of matching energy, resulting in the generation of heat, which is released through a relaxation process [24]. On the other hand, flexible shell of CDs possesses a diverse array of solvophilic and solvophobic functional groups, which provide CDs with versatility [16,25,26], modifiability [27–29], and further supramolecular assembly to aggregates [30,31]. Solvophobic [32–34] effect and hydrogen bond (H-Bond) [35,36], which includes a series of binding energies ranging from weak to strong, are special nocolvant interaction. The synergistic effects of solvophobic effects and H-bonding interactions present a valuable opportunity for the construction and stabilization of CDs aggregates [37–39]. In addition, construction of CDs aggregates through this noncovalent interaction formation can greatly expand the π-conjugate system, promote particle synergy and consequently improve the overall PCE [40,41]. Furthermore, the complex inner hole/slit/channel structure of CDs aggregates enhances the multiple scattering of light from a mesoscopic perspective, thereby improving the rate of light utilization and reducing reflection losses [42,43]. Taking into account both the microscopic and mesoscopic aspects, the CDs aggregates could provide a solid foundation for achieving more efficient photothermal conversion.

In our previous work [44], the CDs with red emission (RCDs) was synthesized by solvothermal method. Herein, ARCDs, which are aggregates of RCDs, was constructed via its solvophobic effects and H-bonding interactions of adjacent RCDs. The photothermal conversion test revealed that these aggregates exhibited an enhanced photothermal conversion effect, reaching a maximum temperature of 116 °C and PCE of 62%. This improvement can be attributed to the synergistic effect among the particles within the aggregates. Consequently, photothermal material (ARCDs@PA), which involves blending and light-curing of ARCDs with polyacrylates (PA), is constructed and loaded on semiconductors to build a photothermoelectric generator (LHE). While LHE has previously unheard-of output efficiency (Voutput ≈ 5000 mV, Ioutput ≈ 25 mA, and Poutput ≈ 123 mW), it can be utilized to power light-emitting diode (LED) lamps in laboratory and to directly charge solar-driven smartphones outdoors, which opening up new possibilities for the development of sustainable energy conversion ( Scheme 1).

RCDs were synthesized by an solventhermal method from citric acid (CA) and benzoylurea (BU) at 180 °C for 6 h following our previously report (see Supporting Information (SI) for details too) [44]. The substantial variation in behaviors of absorption (Fig. S1) and PL (Fig. S2) of RCDs were shown due to the abundant hydrophilic and lipophilic functional groups in the shell of RCDs. Therefore, H2O and DMSO, which have the most obvious differences, are selected as model solvents for comparative study to investigate the essential reasons for these phenomena. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a) (purple trace) and Fig. S3, RCDs exhibit a characteristic absorption peak at 550 nm when dispersed in H2O and the intensity of the main peak shows a linear correlation with concentration (Fig. S4). Conversely, in DMSO, the characteristic peak is redshifted to 628 nm, accompanied by acromion peaks at 585 nm and 715 nm, with a noticeable enhancement in intensity (Fig. 1 (a) (blue trace) & Fig. 1 (b)). The distinct absorption behavior of RCDs in H2O and DMSO can be more clearly identified through normalized absorption spectrum (Fig. S5). It is well known that changes in absorption signify variations in the structure or existence state of CDs [45,46].

More notably, as shown in Fig. 1 (b), the main peak is 580 nm when RCDs are dispersed in DMSO at a low concentration (< 3 μg/mL), but the peak at 630 nm suddenly appears and gradually becomes the new main peak when the concentration rises to ≥ 3 μg/mL, accompanied by the color transition of the solution from light pink to dark blue (see also inset of Fig. 1 (a)). We speculate that the interaction between DMSO and RCDs in dilution is mainly reflected in the shell of independent particles, on one hand, which leads to the red shift of absorption peak from 550 nm (characteristic peak in H2O) to 585 nm (characteristic peak in DMSO). But the interaction between particles cannot be ignored with the increase of its concentration to a certain extent. On the other hand, strong H-bond receptor like DMSO currently act as the bridge to connect various particles, which directly modifies the current state of RCDs and drastically changes their absorption characteristics. This change is likewise reflected in the PL behavior of RCDs (Fig. S6-S9). It can be observed that a new emission peak at 650 nm appeared in the high concentration, gradually becoming the main peak in the PL spectrum (Fig. 1 (c)).

Recent work shows that CDs aggregates, such as supra-CDs, cause red shifts in absorption and PL[40,47]. Therefore, further insights to verify our conjecture can be obtained through the use of a transmission electron microscope (TEM). RCDs are uniformly dispersed and round-like when dispersed in DMSO (1 μg/mL), with an average particle size of ∼1.4 nm and the lattice spacing of ∼0.19 nm (Fig. 1 (d)). However, noticeable irregular particle aggregation was observed throughout the entire field of view when the RCDs was increased to 5 μg/mL (Fig. 1 (e)). The estimated size after aggregation was ∼4.4 nm, while the lattice spacing remained largely unchanged (∼0.19 nm). The results indicate that the interaction between the distinctive amphiphilic shell structure of RCDs, which is similar to previous reports[48–50], is the primary driving mechanism behind their concentration-dependent aggregation. The shell specifically comprises both DMSO-philic H-bond donors and DMSO-phobic aromatic groups. This amphiphilic structure effectively moves hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups under the impact of H-bond interactions, which leading to the formation of aggregates driven by solvent-phobic interactions ultimately (Fig. 1 (f)).

To gain a better understanding of the optical mechanism of red-emitting CDs (RCDs) after forming aggregates in DMSO, we investigated the emission kinetics using time-resolved fluorescence measurements. The PL decay curves was fitted by a di-exponential function, revealing a PL lifetime of 2.17 ns for RCDs aggregates (Fig. S10), which was greatly improved compared to unaggregated RCDs (Fig. S11). It is well established that electron transfer interactions can effectively quench the PL of CDs showing short lifetime when dispersed in a solvent [51,52]. However, the aggregates formed through the synergistic effects of H-bonding interaction and solvophobic effect can protect CDs from quenching, resulting in longer PL lifetimes.

Then, transient absorption (TA) spectra of RCDs aggregates were investigated with 550 nm pulsed laser to further know the changes of excited state energy structure and relaxation process. The 3D TA spectra of RCDs aggregates with probe wavelengths of 500–800 nm and scan delay times of 50 fs to 5.5 ns are depicted in Fig. 2 (a) and (Fig. S12). It can be observed that strong negative signal, namely ground state bleaching (GSB), is at 550 nm ∼ 675 nm, 700 nm ∼ 750 nm and 770 nm, while the excited state absorption (ESA) signal almost disappears, which is difficult to identify (Fig. 2 (b) & Fig. S13). This may be attributed to the energy dissipation in the internal transformation process between particles in the aggregates, which leads to the inability of the excited state to absorb energy again.

The decay kinetics at different wavelengths were monitored to clarify the carrier dynamics of RCDs aggregates (Fig. 2 (c)). The decay kinetic curve was fitted with a di-exponential function. The carrier decay of RCDs aggregates is about 3.1 ns and 2.1 ps, corresponding to two decays in Fig. 2 (c), rather than one decay of the non-aggregates of RCDs (Fig. S14). Furthermore, considering that the excitation energy was higher than the GSB, it is anticipated that the excitons were produced at a lower energy level. Thus, the fast component (2.1 ps) of the GSB recovery dynamics for RCDs aggregates corresponds to the non-radiative electron relaxation process. The slow component (3.1 ns) was most likely due to the recombination process to provide preparation for PL emission [53]. New and longer lifetime component of RCDs aggregates were notably observed (Fig. 2 (c) & Fig. S14), which was attributed to the formation of new relaxation channels due to the H-Bonds and aggregation. The above results are consistent with the analysis of PL lifetimes.

Next, ARCDs were prepared by freeze-drying RCDs in DMSO (Fig. 2 (d), see the preparation process in SI) to RCDs aggregates in the solid state. ARCDs exhibited a distinct peak of S 2p in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which was clearly attributed to the introduction of DMSO following deconvolution (Fig. 2 (e) & Fig. S15). This implies that DMSO was partially anchored to the shell of ARCDs through supramolecular interactions, rather than being completely removed. Meanwhile, X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S16) and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S17) could also prove more clearly that the aggregation does not alter the crystalline state of the carbon core. This further substantiates the occurrence of aggregation between the shells of the particles. Comparing the results of RCDs and ARCDs, small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) show that the ordered structure of RCDs was broken after aggregation, showing wider and smoother signal peaks (Fig. 2 (f)), implying irregular aggregation, which is consistent with TEM observations.

Furthermore, observing H-bonding interactions in the RCDs aggregates (ARCDs) is essential to validate the proposed mechanism that suggests a collaboration between H-bonding interaction and solvophobic effect, which together contribute to aggregation as previously described. Therefore, we investigated the presence of H-Bonds through FTIR, and DSC spectroscopy.

As shown in Fig. 2 (g), we focus on the three bands (1. 3300 ∼ 3000 cm−1, 2. 1700 ∼ 1500 cm−1 and 3. 1200 ∼ 750 cm−1) with the most obvious changes in the FTIR spectrum. Comparison results in the first part show that the peak of N-H in ARCDs is wider and the intensity is lower than RCDs, which may be due to DMSO, a strong H-bond receptor, which destroys the N-H association of CDs and makes Ar-H move to higher wavenumber. In the second part, the amide I bond (CO) has no obvious change, while the amide II bond (N-H) has a red shift in wavenumber, which can also be attributed to the H-bonding between SO and N-H, resulting red shift. Strong and sharp three peaks are more evidence of the effect of H-bonding on the C-N and C-O vibrational peaks and one of the evidences for the presence of DMSO as a complement to the XPS characterization. FTIR spectra strongly demonstrate the effect of DMSO as a strong H-bond receptor on the shell of CDs.

Moreover, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) is used to further prove the existence of H-Bonds (Fig. 2 (h)) [54]. The endothermic transition of ARCDs at about 100 °C is attributed to the break of H-Bonds between CDs, while this endothermic transition was not observed in RCDs. Therefore, H-bond interaction between the shell structure and DMSO is indeed present in ARCDs, thereby providing strong evidence that our conjecture regarding the aggregation mechanism is accurate. While the fluorescence lifetime is unnoticeable due to the dramatic drop in PL quantum yiled (PLQY), the absorption spectra of ARCDs is expanded, clearly red-shifted, and greatly enhanced when compared to RCDs (Equa. S1 & S2 and Tab. S1), which lays a solid foundation for efficient photothermal conversion (corresponding Jablonsky diagram (Fig. S18) is detailed in SI).

We initially investigated the photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of ARCDs by using 808 nm laser, because aggregation greatly promoted the NIR absorption (see the experimental section for details). When the concentration increased from 0 to 160 μg/mL, the temperature of ARCDs increased by 55 °C, within 10 min under a power density of 1 W/cm2 ( Fig. 3 (a) & Fig. S19). Additionally, temperature increased by 89 °C, when the ARCDs (160 μg/mL) were irradiated with an 808 nm laser, within 10 min as the power density increased from 0 to 1.4 W/cm2 (Fig. 3 (b) & Fig. S20). Under the same conditions, the temperature of pure DMSO increased by less than 3 °C. Notably, the maximum temperature of the ARCDs reached 116 °C in just 6 min under the conditions of 160 μg/mL and 1.4 W/cm2 (Fig. 3 (c)). Furthermore, we calculated the PCE of ARCDs to be 62 % (Equa. S3 & Tab. S2) [55,56], surpassing the majority of reported CDs, which lays a solid foundation for subsequent photothermal applications. The enhanced PCE upon aggregation stems from dual-scale synergy. Microscopically, ARCDs via H-bonding and solvophobic assembly expand π-conjugation and enhance interparticle interactions; mesoscopically, ARCDs' hierarchical pore networks boost light-trapping through multiscale scattering while minimizing reflection.

Controllable heating and excellent stability are important factors for evaluating CDs photothermal materials. The increase of temperature is positively correlated with the laser irradiation time of ARCDs, indicating that the heat generation can be well controlled (Fig. S21 & S22). In addition, it is worth noting that the ARCDs show excellent thermal stability and photostability. After heating and cooling for four cycles under continuous laser irradiation, the temperature remains stable without degradation and agglomeration (Fig. 3 (d)).

CDs have been widely used as a photothermal conversion material in various applications such as photothermal therapy and photothermal sterilization [57,58]. However, achieving photothermalelectrical continuous energy conversion remains a challenge. Therefore, we attempted to blend ARCDs with polyacrylate (PA) and cured the mixed material using initiator under the irradiation of 365 nm lamp ( Fig. 4 (a)). This resulted in the preparation of ARCDs@PA photothermal material, which are more porous in comparison to pure PA. The structural characteristics of the composites were clearly observed in the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images (Fig. 4 (b) & Fig. S23). This could be attributed to the embedding of ARCDs within the network structure of PA, forming connections through carbonyl and ester bonds via H-bonds interaction (Fig. 4 (a)). This disruption of the uniform and continuous cross-linking structure led to the formation of porous. The ARCDs@PA nanosheets for TEM characterization were prepared through ultrasonic fragmentation. Microscopic analysis reveals quasi-circular architectures with an average diameter of ∼300 nm (Fig. 4 (c)). Higher magnification imaging demonstrates homogeneous dispersion of ARCDs across PA substrates, while concentration-dependent assembly processes lead to enlarged aggregates averaging ∼18 nm in size. These structural crucially observations provide definitive evidence for the encapsulation of ARCDs. In addition, DSC analysis showed ARCDs@PA's melting temperature depression relative to PA (Fig. S24), induced by ARCDs disrupting polymer ordering. This structural disruption mechanism—consistent with SEM-observed porosity—was further supported by TGA data while both materials share similar degradation patterns, as shown in Fig. S25, ARCDs@PA demonstrates superior thermal stability with an onset decomposition temperature elevated to ~200 °C. We conducted further measurements on the absorption spectra of PA and ARCDs@PA. Our findings reveal that ARCDs@PA displays a notably higher absorbance across a broad wavelength range (200–1600 nm) compared to PA (Fig. 4 (d)). The absorbance spectrum of ARCDs@PA aligns well with the solar spectrum. These results clearly highlight the potential of ARCDs@PA as a sustainable solar absorber for photothermal conversion.

Subsequently, ARCDs@PA was loaded onto semiconductors and transformed into a photothermoelectric generator (Fig. S26 (a) & (b)), namely LHE, meaning continuous transfer from Light, to Heat, further to Electric energy. It should be mentioned that the two LHE are effectively connected in series here in order to improve performance from the perspective of engineering (Fig. S26 (c)). By simulating solar light and illuminating the LHEs with a Xenon lamp instead of an 808 nm laser, solar-driven technology is achieved, hence expanding the range of situations in which the LHE can be used. At the same time of illumination, the temperatures of semiconductors (without any medium), semiconductors loaded with blank, pure PA and ARCDs@PA were 25.6 °C, 25.9 °C, and 64.9 °C, respectively (Fig. 4 (e)). This represents a 30-fold acceleration in thermal response compared to recently CDs-based photothermal materials, which typically require > 20 min to reach equivalent thermal gradients under energy conversion protocols [17,59]. It is noteworthy that ARCDs@PA exhibits a high PCE and can achieve a maximum temperature of 64.9 °C within 40 s. In comparison, RCDs@PA shows a temperature rise of less than 30 °C (Fig. S27), which could be attributed to the blending uniformity of RCDs and PA. In addition, the maximum temperature attained by ARCDs@PA is not as high as the results obtained from the solution test mentioned earlier. This discrepancy may be attributed to the change in light source and the difference between the composite material and pure CDs. However, this discrepancy does not affect the subsequent application.

Furthermore, the stability of ARCDs@PA was evaluated from three aspects: circulating switching light source, continuous irradiation for a long time, and reuse at sufficient intervals. After 10 cycles, the temperature difference (ΔT) of ARCDs@PA remained stable above 40 °C (Fig. S28). The thermal stability of ARCDs@PA is still excellent, without remarkable attenuation, even after two consecutive irradiation for 30 min (Fig. S29), which lays the foundation for long time work outdoor. More importantly, when we tested ARCDs@PA again after placing it in ambient conditions (average temperature ≈ 20 °C, average humidity ≈ 68 %) for a month, its ΔT can still reach 40 °C (Fig. S30), which means that the module does not require stringent storage conditions, paving the way for future practical transformation road.

As the irradiation time is prolonged, the voltage and current gradually increase when LHEs is exposed to sunlight intensity (100 mW/cm2) (supplementary video-1). The growth trend of temperature and output power remains consistent. Furthermore, continuous illumination leads to continuous heat generation and electricity production. The working principle of LHE and the voltage and current test diagram are illustrated in Fig. 4 (f). In addition, we were able to successfully light two LED bulbs using LHEs, as illustrated in Fig. S31 and supplementary video-2. Furthermore, we attempted to charge a mobile phone (Fig. S32) and achieved continuous illumination and successful charging. The fact that we position the LHEs immediately on the lawn outdoor during sunny weather just heightens our excitement since the mobile phone can be charged instantly once we connect the LHEs to it after LHEs has been in the sun for ∼ 1 min (Fig. 4 (g) and supplementary video-3). Then, the average voltage as ∼ 4.97 V and the average current as ∼ 16.27 mA via a multimeter (inset of Fig. 4 (g)). The remarkable output power originates principally from the synergistic coupling between ARCDs@PA's enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency and rational device architecture engineering. This presents a new sustainable concept for the advancement of outdoor portable power supply systems and offers new perspectives on the application of CDs in photothermal conversion. To our knowledge, this is the first report on improving the ability of photothermal conversion and achieving photothermoelectric generator by regulating the aggregation of CDs.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S3.
Video S3

In addition, the stability of LHE devices is evaluated by multiple measurements of its voltage, current, and power. In 10 consecutive test cycles, the output voltage (Fig. S33) and current (Fig. S34) of LHEs device have almost no decay, and can maintain excellent stability. The maximum voltage is constant at 5 V, the current is close to 25 mA, and the calculated rated power can be stabilized at about 123 mW (Fig. 4 (h)). Likewise, the output power remained stable during two consecutive irradiations for 30 min (Fig. S35–37). In the test with an interval of one month, LHEs still performed well (Fig. S38–40). More importantly, to the best of our knowledge, compared with the work published so far on photothermoelectric generator, LHE exhibits excellent photothermoelectric conversion performance (Fig. 4 (i) and Tab. S4). Obviously, excellent stability and outstanding performance have created a strong basis for LHE's transition from lab to industry.

In summary, excellent photothermal conversion modules (PCE ≈ 62%) were constructed using ARCDs (aggregates of RCDs), synergistically induced by its solvophobic effect and H-bonding interaction, which significantly improved absorption of solar light. These modules were then loaded onto semiconductors to create solar-driven photothermoelectric generator (LHE), which successfully charged smartphones in an outdoor environment with efficiency and stability. Our study demonstrates the potential for sustainable use of solar-driven photothermoelectric generators, which offer answers of CDs for the impending energy issue and growing environmental consciousness. Furthermore, we offer a supramolecular chemical approach for the study of CDs assembly by demonstrating the vigor of supramolecular interaction in generating CDs assembly. Our group is systematically exploring the structure-activity correlations in CDs aggregates to optimize their energy conversion efficiency, while concurrently advancing the engineering of RCDs-enabled smart window technologies and building-integrated photothermal-electrical hybrid energy systems.

Shan Feishi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yan Li: Investigation. Wei Zhihong: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Liao Chengshuang: Investigation. Li Jing: Investigation. Wang Zhouyu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Tian Yuxi: Writing – review & editing. Wang Leyong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21871136), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2023NSFSC1977, 2022ZYD0048), Sichuan Provincial College Student Innovation Training Program (S202210623049, 202310623017), and the Starry Night Science Fund of Zhejiang University Shanghai Institute for Advanced Study, Grant No. SN-ZJU-SIAS-006. We thank Mr. Chengquan Shan, a veteran electricity enthusiast, for his help in making the LHE conversion model. We thank Chengdu Zhichuangxin Advertising Co., Ltd for the manufacture of LED Lamp too.

The enhanced photothermal effect of graphene/conjugated polymer composites: photoinduced energy transfer and applications in photocontrolled switches. Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 14345-14348, https://doi.org/10.1039/c4cc06849a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor microenvironment stimuli-responsive fluorescence imaging and synergistic cancer therapy by carbon-dot–Cu2+ nanoassemblies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sun; Q. Chen; Z. Tang; C. Liu; Z. Li; A. Wu; H. Lin. Tumor Microenvironment Stimuli-Responsive Fluorescence Imaging and Synergistic Cancer Therapy by Carbon-Dot–Cu2+ Nanoassemblies. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 21041-21048, https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.202007786.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared phosphorescent carbon dots for sonodynamic precision tumor therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Geng; J. Hu; Y. Li; S. Feng; D. Pan; L. Feng; L. Shen. Near-infrared phosphorescent carbon dots for sonodynamic precision tumor therapy. Nature Commun. 13 (2022) 5735, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-33474-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of interfacial interactions and complexation of carbon dots to construct ultra-robust and efficient photothermal film from micro-carbonized polysaccharides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ding; S. Liu; L. Yang; G. Du; J. Wan; Z. Chen; S. Li. Use of Interfacial Interactions and Complexation of Carbon Dots to Construct Ultra-Robust and Efficient Photothermal Film From Micro-Carbonized Polysaccharides. Small 20 (2024) 2401942, https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202401942.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Feishi Shan is a doctoral candidate in Organic Chemistry at Nanjing University. His research centers on the structural elucidation and functional engineering of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) at the mesoscopic scale, with particular emphasis on their exceptional performance in energy conversion systems and biomedical technologies.
Li Yan obtained her Master's degree from Xihua University, during which she concentrated on the design of carbon dots (CDs) with photodynamic therapeutic functionalities.

Zhihong Wei is currently a Postdoctoral Researcher at Nanjing University. He obtained his Ph.D. in 2022 from Nanjing University, where he specialized in the study of single-molecule spectroscopy and the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs). His research focuses on the fundamental understanding of the photophysical behavior of CDs.

Chengshuang Liao is presently a graduate student at Xihua University, with her research primarily focusing on the design, synthesis, and practical applications of functional carbonized polymer dots (CPDs).

Jing Li received his PhD degree from the Institute of Semiconductor, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in 2009. He subsequently held a postdoctoral research fellowship at the Institute of Physics, CAS. From 2011, Dr. Li joined the Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, CAS. His current research interests include the application and the technology development of ultrafast spectroscopy.

Zhouyu Wang received her Ph.D. degree from Chengdu Institute of Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2007. Now she is a full professor of Xihua University, and her current research focuses on carbon dots (especially carbonized polymer dots) and organic synthesis.

Yuxi Tian received his B.S. from Peking University in 2003 and his PhD in 2008 from Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science. From 2009–2015, he did postdoctoral research in Lund University, Sweden and Leiden University, Netherlands. In 2015, he joined Nanjing University as a professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering. He is current research interest is single molecule spectroscopy, including single molecule devices, photo physics in photoelectric materials and development of new technologies.

Leyong Wang received his Ph.D. from Nanjing Univ. in 2000. After postdoctoral stay at Institute of Chemistry, CAS with Prof. Youliang Hu, Université de Bourgogne with Dr. Jean-Claude Chambron and Prof. Roger Guilard, and Mainz Univ. with Dr. Voker Böhmer, he then became a Humboldt Research Fellow in Erlangen-Nürnberg Univ., affiliated with Professor John Gladysz. In 2006, he settled in Nanjing. He has been serving on the (advisory) editorial boards of numerous journals, including RSC Adv., Chem. Soc. Rev., Coord. Chem. Rev.; Chin. Chem. Lett. et al. He has authored or coauthored over 260 papers in international peer reviewed journals.
